coredata.dc:title: Synthesis and modification of pristine and nitrogen-doped carbon dots by combining template pyrolysis and oxidation
coredata.dc:creator: Chernyak, Sergei
Podgornova, Angelina
Dorofeev, Sergey
Maksimov, Sergey
Maslakov, Konstantin
Savilov, Serguei
Lunin, Valery
coredata.dc:description: The template pyrolysis synthesis, as a bottom-up approach, and oxidation, as a top-down approach, were combined to produce pristine and nitrogen doped carbon dots. Varying the precursor type, synthesis temperature and oxidation time the color of carbon dots was tuned from green to orange because of the bandgap changes and formation of interband states. TEM, Raman, and XPS data revealed the graphene core of carbon dots with the shell of carboxyl and hydroxyl functionalities. The bulk and surface N-doping of the carbon dots affected their photoluminescence in a different way because of the opposite effect on the π-electron system: the core modification led to a blue shift, while the surface amination resulted in a red shift. A blue shift was also observed for the undoped dots with increasing the synthesis temperature, while more prolonged oxidation led to a red shift. Excitation with different wavelengths revealed the inhomogeneity of photoluminescence sites in nitrogen-doped carbon dots.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Graphene quantum dots
N-doping
Photoluminescence
Template pyrolysis

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of H-800 (a, b) and A-800 (c, d) GNFs.
Survey (a), C1s (b), N1s (c) XPS and Raman (d) spectra of GNFs and CDs; SEM image of dried H-900-7 (e); TEM images of H-800-3 (f, g).

UV-vis adsorption spectra of CDs (a); normalized PL spectra of CDs (b); optical images of H-900-7 (left) and H-900-3 (right) under visible light (c) and with 406 nm laser excitation (d); excitation and PL spectra of H-900-3 (e) and A-800-2 (f) at different excitation wavelengths.

head.ce:title: Synthesis and modification of pristine and nitrogen-doped carbon dots by combining template pyrolysis and oxidation

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Lomonosov Moscow State University, Leninskie Gory 1-3, Moscow 119991, Russia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Lomonosov Moscow State University, Leninskie Gory 1-3, Moscow 119991, Russia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The template pyrolysis synthesis, as a bottom-up approach, and oxidation, as a top-down approach, were combined to produce pristine and nitrogen doped carbon dots. Varying the precursor type, synthesis temperature and oxidation time the color of carbon dots was tuned from green to orange because of the bandgap changes and formation of interband states. TEM, Raman, and XPS data revealed the graphene core of carbon dots with the shell of carboxyl and hydroxyl functionalities. The bulk and surface N-doping of the carbon dots affected their photoluminescence in a different way because of the opposite effect on the π-electron system: the core modification led to a blue shift, while the surface amination resulted in a red shift. A blue shift was also observed for the undoped dots with increasing the synthesis temperature, while more prolonged oxidation led to a red shift. Excitation with different wavelengths revealed the inhomogeneity of photoluminescence sites in nitrogen-doped carbon dots.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are one of the youngest members of the family of carbon nanomaterials. Typically, CDs consist of a carbon core of less than 10–20 nm in diameter covered with functional groups. Water solubility, bleaching resistance, and nontoxicity distinguish them from semiconductor quantum dots [1]. Depending on the core structure, CDs can be divided onto graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with the 2D-layered graphene core, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with the spherical layered core, and carbon nanodots (CNDs) with the amorphous core [2]. Among them GQDs stand out for their sp2-conjugated structure, unique electronic properties, complex bandgap structure, catalytic action, and high thermal conductivity, but their scalable production is still an issue [2,3].

Tunable photoluminescent properties of CDs are important for their application in bio-imaging, light‐emitting diodes, full‐color displays [4–6]. Different synthetic approaches are applied to obtain CDs of different colors. In the case of bottom-up strategy a variation of the precursor type or careful separation of the CD mixture by column chromatography are used. The color of graphene-based photoluminescent materials obtained by a top-down approach can be tuned by varying the size or functionalization degree of sp2-domains [6–9]. Therefore, it should be useful to combine the top-down and bottom-up approaches to develop a powerful and scalable technology for the synthesis of multi-color CDs with the desirable properties.

The template pyrolysis of hydrocarbons is an effective and scalable way to produce undoped and doped graphene nanoflakes (GNFs) of 10–100 nm in diameter [10]. This approach is flexible to synthesize GNF particles of different size, porosity, defectiveness, doping degree, etc. Oxidation of the resulted material immobilizes oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of particles, making their structure similar to GQDs. In this work, we adopt the template pyrolysis synthesis of GNFs to the production of CDs. The developed technique combines both a bottom-up stage in which GNFs of 20 nm in size are grown on the surface of the MgO template and a top-down stage in which the grown GNFs are split into CDs by oxidative treatment. The selection of the precursor type and pyrolysis conditions affects the core structure of the finally produced CDs, which allows tuning their bandgap. Moreover, the top-down synthesis of CDs usually requires a long harsh treatment (24 h or even several days) [6,9,11]. But in our case 3 h of nitric acid treatment was enough, because the size of the treated GNFs was close to the optimal size of CDs and they contained many edge carbon atoms labile to oxidation. In addition, we used two techniques to produce N-doped CDs: the in-situ doping during CVD growth of GNFs and post-synthesis amine functionalization of CDs. These techniques allowed introducing N-atoms either into the core or on the surface of CDs.

The template synthesis of GNFs was described in our previous work [10]. The MgO template was synthesized by long-term (2 days) precipitation of magnesium oxalate followed by calcination at 500 °C. To obtain GNFs, hexane or acetonitrile in a nitrogen gas flow was passed for 15 min over the template placed in a quartz tubular bed reactor heated to 700, 800 or 900 °C. The synthesized materials were washed via refluxing with 1:1 HCl/water solution and filtered with distilled water using a 0.2 µm polypropylene membrane. The GNF samples were named as “P-T”, where P is the precursor type (H – hexane, A – acetonitrile) and T is the synthesis temperature. Pristine (undoped) GNFs were refluxed with nitric acid for 3 and 7 h, while the 2 h treatment was used for N-doped GNFs. For this purpose, 0.5 g of GNFs or N-GNFs was placed into a glass flask and 40 ml of acid was added for refluxing. After that, the solution was neutralized by adding dropwise aqua ammonia 25%, centrifuged at 14000 rpm, and dialyzed for 48 h in a 3.5 kDa MFPI bag against distilled water. Water was changed every 12 h. The scheme of CDs synthesis is depicted in Fig. 1 . The CD samples were named as “P-T-t” where P-T designates the GNFs used as a precursor, while t is the oxidation time. The surface of H-900-3 was modified via refluxing in pure n-BuNH2 for 6 h and the modified sample was named H-900-3-BuNH2.

The morphology and structure of CDs was investigated by SEM and TEM using JSM-6390LA and JEM 2100 F/Cs JEOL microscopes. The surface state of the samples was analyzed by XPS using an Axis Ultra DLD spectrometer (Kratos Analytical Ltd.). The pass energy of the analyzer was 160 eV for survey spectra and 40 eV for high-resolution scans. Raman spectra were registered on a LabRam HR800 UV microscope-spectrometer using 5mW argon laser excitation. The optical properties of CDs were studied with SM2203 (Solar) and USB 4000 (Ocean Optics) UV–vis spectrometers and a Carry 50 (Varian) spectrophotometer.

TEM images (Fig. 2 ) showed that unoxidized GNFs were of about 20 nm in diameter and contained 4–7 graphene layers. The N-doped GNFs were more defective than the pristine ones, which is typical for doped graphene-based materials [12].

The D1 band in the Raman spectra of CDs is broader than that for GNF and shifted to higher wavenumbers (Fig. 3d), which points to the oxidation and amorphization of the GNF edges under nitric acid treatment. The C band at ∼1750 cm−1 corresponds to stretching vibrations of CO bonds in carbonyls and carboxyls [17,18]. The position of this band depends on the nature of groups and their neighboring atoms. Therefore, the broad C band in the Raman spectra of CDs reflects the diversity in carboxyl locations. Though this band is not usually observed in Raman spectra of carbon materials, the high oxygen content in CDs (ca. 30 at.%) made it visible in our case. Interestingly, the intensity ratio of the D and G bands is the same for GNFs and CDs, which can be explained by the strong interaction between individual CDs with the formation of graphite-like macro-structure as it was shown by SEM (Fig. 3e). At the same time, the Raman bands for GNFs synthesized at 800 °C are significantly broader than those for GNFs synthesized at 900 °C. This can be explained by the decrease in the defectiveness of GNFs with increasing the synthesis temperature. According to TEM the synthesized CDs were of 1–6 nm in diameter with the lattice spacing of 0.21 ± 0.03 nm that may attributed to (1 0 0) planes of the graphene core (Fig. 3g, h).

The absorption spectra of CDs (Fig. 4 a) show a rather narrow and intensive peak at 220–240 nm that corresponds to π → π* transitions in the aromatic system of graphene core and functional groups and a broad shoulder at higher wavelengths (higher than 260 nm) with low intensive peaks. This shoulder can be assigned to various n → π* transitions of different functionalities (CO, NO) [2,19]. n → σ* transitions in CN and CO bonds may also contribute to this shoulder [20].

The combination of template pyrolysis and oxidation allowed producing CDs of different colors (Fig. 4b–d). The photoluminescence (PL) spectra of these CDs (Fig. 4b) revealed several trends. First of all, the increase in the pyrolysis temperature of hexane led to a blue shift of PL of CDs from ∼585 nm for H-700-3 to ∼555 nm for H-800-3 and ∼515 nm for H-900-3. Earlier it was shown that the decrease in defectiveness under graphene oxide reduction or hydroxylation of fluorinated graphene led to the blue shift of PL [7,8]. This fact was explained by the increase in both the number and area of sp2-C domains surrounded by sp3 areas. In the case of GQDs the quantum confinement and intrinsic properties of the core plays a crucial role in PL [3]. Actually, the defectiveness of graphene-based materials reduces with increasing the synthesis temperature according to both our Raman results and literature data [21]. We can propose that defects in H-800-3 and H-700-3 suppressed π*→π transitions and stimulate interstate ones.

The smallest Stokes shift among all studied samples was observed for N-doped CDs despite the fact that the highest defectiveness was expected for this sample because of the presence of N-functionalities. In addition, it was earlier proposed that the decrease in graphitization degree of N-doped CDs could lead to a shift of their emission band from yellow to green [22]. Therefore, in the present study the increase in defectiveness led to the different PL shifts depending on the way the defectiveness was changed: by varying the synthesis temperature or by N-doping. To explain this fact we analyzed the PL dependence from the excitation wavelength (Fig. 4e, f). H-900-3 demonstrated the excitation-independent PL with the most intensive excitation band at 350 nm (Fig. 4e). At the same time N-doped CDs showed a strong excitation at 250 nm and a red shift of PL at higher excitation wavelengths indicating the different PL mechanisms for non-doped and N-doped CDs. Apparently, C–N bonds in N-doped CDs (amide groups were detected by XPS) intensified n → σ* transitions shifting PL to higher energies at low excitation wavelengths, while higher excitation wavelengths intensified n → π* transitions leading to a red shift of PL. Thus, the N-doped sample showed a noticeable inhomogeneity of PL active centers.

Surface modification of H-900-3 by n-BuNH2 led to a noticeable red shift of PL (Fig. 4b). There is a lot of controversy in the literature concerning the role of the amine functionalization in the PL shift of GQDs. For example, Zhu et al. found that the amine and amide modifications led to a blue shift of PL and increased its intensity because of the removal of carboxylic and epoxy groups that facilitated non-radiative recombination of localized electron-hole pairs [23]. Moreover, it was pointed that this modification enhanced the π-electron system in GQDs. At the same time, the opposite trends were observed in other works. Both primary and secondary amines on the GQD surface were found to reduce the band gap because of the charge transfer between amines and GQDs, which was also supported by the computational experiments [24,25]. The latter results seem to be closer to our work and can be used to explain the red shift of PL after modification of H-900-3 with butylamine. Amine fragments can cling to carboxyl and hydroxyl groups forming tails of secondary amines and amides, enhancing donor-acceptor mechanism of charge transfer, and lowering the band gap of GQDs [24] or forming interband states [26].

Interestingly, when N-fragments were synthesized inside the core of GQDs the blue shift of PL was observed compared to the undoped dots [27]. This agrees with our results for the CVD N-doped sample. At the same time, according to the XPS data most of nitrogen in our case was in amide groups in contrast to pyridinic nitrogen in work [27]. The authors explained the blue shift by the “relatively strong electron affinity of N atoms” in the N-GQDs. According to our results, bulk and surface N-doping of GQDs by amines and amides led to the opposite PL shift, which is rather interesting. We can assume that the bulk doping of GQDs with oxidized N fragments increased their band gap because of the degradation of the π-electron system while the opposite trend was observed under their surface modification.

We have demonstrated that the scalable template pyrolysis synthesis of GNFs followed by their oxidation allows producing pristine and N-doped graphene quantum dots. Their color can be tuned from green to orange by varying synthesis and post-treatment conditions. The proposed technique stands out among others by the possibility to tune the defectiveness and doping degree of graphene core of CDs at the CVD stage, while the small size of the synthesized GNFs allows using short oxidation time.

The increase in the GNF synthesis temperature blueshifted the PL of CDs because less defects were formed in graphene layers at higher temperatures. Two N-doping techniques were used to produce N-CDs: the in situ CVD growth of N-GNFs from acetonitrile and post-synthesis treatment with Butylamine to immobilize amine fragments on the surface of dots. These techniques affected the PL peak position in a different way. Bulk doping introduced defects into the graphene core of CDs and increased their bandgap while the surface modification added donor-acceptor amine and amide fragments and enhanced charge transfer between functional groups and graphene core reducing the bandgap.

Sergei Chernyak: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing, Funding acquisition. Angelina Podgornova: Investigation, Methodology. Sergey Dorofeev: Investigation. Sergey Maksimov: Investigation. Konstantin Maslakov: Investigation, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing. Serguei Savilov: Supervision, Resources, Writing - review & editing. Valery Lunin: Supervision, Writing - review & editing, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank Dr. O.Y. Isaikina for Raman spectroscopy study. This work was supported by the Russian Science Foundation under grant No. 18–73–00061. The authors acknowledge support from “Nanochemistry and Nanomaterials” MSU Equipment Center acting under Lomonosov Moscow State University Program of Development.
